content.problemStatement:
You are given an array A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \dots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ of length NNN that may contain duplicates. Find all possible unique permutations of AAA.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN - the length of the array AAA.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1,A_2,\ldots,A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​.


Output Format
For each test case, output M+1M+1M+1 lines, where MMM is the number of unique permutations:



The first line contains a single integer MMM.
The next MMM lines contain NNN space-separated integers each which will be permutation of AAA.

Note: The permutations must be printed in **lexicographically increasing** order.

Permutatios a1,a2,…,aNa_1,a_2,\ldots,a_Na1​,a2​,…,aN​ is said to be lexicographically smaller than permutation b1,b2,…,bNb_1,b_2,\ldots,b_Nb1​,b2​,…,bN​ if there exists a position iii where ai<bia_i \lt b_iai​<bi​ and aj=bja_j = b_jaj​=bj​ for all j<ij \lt ij<i.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer N, the length of the array. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1 … AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output M+1 lines where M is the number of unique permutations of the given array. The first line contains M. The next M lines each contain a permutation as N space‑separated integers. Permutations must be printed in lexicographically increasing order.

solution.approach: Sort the input array. Use a depth‑first backtracking that builds the permutation one position at a time. Keep a boolean used[] array. When choosing the next element, skip an element if it is equal to the previous element and the previous element has not been used in the current position – this avoids generating duplicate permutations. Whenever the current permutation reaches length N, store it. After the search finishes, the stored permutations are already in lexicographic order because we always iterate over the sorted array from left to right. Finally print the count and all permutations.
solution.timeComplexity: O(M·N) where M is the number of unique permutations (M ≤ N! and much smaller when duplicates exist).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(M·N) to store all permutations plus O(N) recursion stack and auxiliary arrays.
